textEn:
Change the given sentence to its direct speech.

She said that she stays in a hostel.
textHi:
Change the given sentence to its direct speech.

She said that she stays in a hostel.
optionsEn: She said, "She stay in a hostel."
She said, "I stay in a hostel."
She said, I stay in a hostel.
She said that, "I stay in a hostel."
explanationEn:
The correct answer is She said, "I stay in a hostel."

Key Points

  • Given sentence "She said that she stays in a hostel" is in Simple Present Tense and in Indirect speech.
  • The rule for changing a Simple Present Indefinite tense form into an Direct Speech is as follows:
    • That is replaced with inverted commas.
    • Tense of the sentence remains the same. In the given sentence, Simple Present Tense will remain same while converting into Direct Speech.
      • Structure: Subject + Inverted commas + Object. (Direct Speech)
        • Example: She says, "She lives in the New Delhi."
      • Structure: Subject + That + Object. (Indirect Speech)
        • Example: She said that she lived in the New Delhi.

The correct sentence is: She said, "I stay in a hostel."

Additional Information

  • Direct speech is used to describe what someone has stated. When we just repeat someone else's words, enclosing the phrase in speech marks, we are using direct speaking.
  • Indirect speech is used to report what someone said without speech bubbles and without necessarily using their exact words. It is also called reported speech.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is She said, "I stay in a hostel."

Key Points

  • Given sentence "She said that she stays in a hostel" is in Simple Present Tense and in Indirect speech.

    • Tense of the sentence remains the same. In the given sentence, Simple Present Tense will remain same while converting into Direct Speech.
      • Structure: Subject + Inverted commas + Object. (Direct Speech)
        • Example: She says, "She lives in the New Delhi."

        • Example: She said that she lived in the New Delhi.

The correct sentence is: She said, "I stay in a hostel."

S1: Things are often not what they seem.

S6: This happened without you even knowing it. So imagine the changes that occur to this earth and humanity.

P: But you are really not, because the Milky Way galaxy, of which you are a part, is moving through space at 2.1 million kilometres an hour.

Q: So in roughly twenty seconds that it would have taken you to read this paragraph, you have already moved thousands of kilometre.

R: And that is without taking into account the effects of earth's rotation on its own axis, its orbiting around the sun and sun's journey around the Milky Way.

S: As you read this sentence, perhaps sitting in a comfortable chair in your study, you would probably consider yourself at rest.

The correct sequence should be:
textHi:
Directions: In this section, each item consists of six sentences of a passage. The first sentences in each sentence are given in the beginning as S1 and S6. The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labeled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet.

S1: Things are often not what they seem.

S6: This happened without you even knowing it. So imagine the changes that occur to this earth and humanity.

P: But you are really not, because the Milky Way galaxy, of which you are a part, is moving through space at 2.1 million kilometres an hour.

Q: So in roughly twenty seconds that it would have taken you to read this paragraph, you have already moved thousands of kilometre.

R: And that is without taking into account the effects of earth's rotation on its own axis, its orbiting around the sun and sun's journey around the Milky Way.

S: As you read this sentence, perhaps sitting in a comfortable chair in your study, you would probably consider yourself at rest.

The correct sequence should be:
explanationEn:
The correct answer is S, P, R, Q​.

  • An introductory clause is already given in the question that mentions the topic 'Things being different from the common perception'.
  • ​​Sentence S tells us the perception of us being at rest. It will be put in the first place.
  • Sentence P addresses the actual scenario where we are moving at around 2.1 kilometres per hour. It will be put in second place.
  • Sentence R takes the story forward by mentioning the factors which aren't included in calculating the above-mentioned speed. It will be put in third place.
  • The last sentence is Q as it mentions the distance that we might have traveled while reading the current paragraph.

Thus, the correct order is: Things are often not what they seem. As you read this sentence, perhaps sitting in a comfortable chair in your study, you would probably consider yourself at rest. But you are really not, because the Milky Way galaxy, of which you are a part, is moving through space at 2.1 million kilometres an hour. And that is without taking into account the effects of earth's rotation on its own axis, its orbiting around the sun and the sun's journey around the Milky Way. So in roughly twenty seconds that it would have taken you to read this paragraph, you have already moved thousands of kilometre. This happened without you even knowing it. So imagine the changes that occur to this earth and humanity.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is S, P, R, Q​.

  • An introductory clause is already given in the question that mentions the topic 'Things being different from the common perception'.
  • ​​Sentence S tells us the perception of us being at rest. It will be put in the first place.
  • Sentence P addresses the actual scenario where we are moving at around 2.1 kilometres per hour. It will be put in second place.
  • Sentence R takes the story forward by mentioning the factors which aren't included in calculating the above-mentioned speed. It will be put in third place.
  • The last sentence is Q as it mentions the distance that we might have traveled while reading the current paragraph.

Thus, the correct order is: Things are often not what they seem. As you read this sentence, perhaps sitting in a comfortable chair in your study, you would probably consider yourself at rest. But you are really not, because the Milky Way galaxy, of which you are a part, is moving through space at 2.1 million kilometres an hour. And that is without taking into account the effects of earth's rotation on its own axis, its orbiting around the sun and the sun's journey around the Milky Way. So in roughly twenty seconds that it would have taken you to read this paragraph, you have already moved thousands of kilometre. This happened without you even knowing it. So imagine the changes that occur to this earth and humanity.

textEn:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. In case no word is needed, select 'No word required'.

Vinod's car is ________ most expensive one in our neighbourhood.
textHi:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank. In case no word is needed, select 'No word required'.

Vinod's car is ________ most expensive one in our neighbourhood.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'the'.
Key Points
  • The article "the" is used to specify a particular item that is known to both the speaker and the listener.
  • In this context, "the most expensive one" specifies a particular car that is the most expensive in the neighbourhood.
  • The other options: "a," and "an" do not fit the context as appropriately. "No word required" is also not suitable as the sentence requires a definite article.

​Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 2'.

Complete Sentence: Vinod's car is the most expensive one in our neighbourhood.

Additional Information
  • A (एक): is an indefinite article used before words that begin with a consonant sound.
  • An (एक): is an indefinite article used before words that begin with a vowel sound.
  • No word required: suggests that the sentence does not need an article, which is incorrect in this context.

As the world becomes more connected threw the internet,(A)/ the importance of cybersecurity(B)/ has grown exponentially.(C)/ No error(D)
textHi:
Directions: Each item in this section has a sentence with three underlined parts labelled as (a), (b) and (c). Read each sentence to find out whether there is any error in any underlined part and indicate your response. on the Answer Sheet against the corresponding letter i.e., (a) or (b) or (c). If you find no error, your response should be indicated as (d).

As the world becomes more connected threw the internet,(A)/ the importance of cybersecurity(B)/ has grown exponentially.(C)/ No error(D)
explanationEn:
The correct answer is "A"

Key Points

  • There is an error in part (a). The correct phrase should be: "As the world becomes more connected through the internet"
  • The preposition "through" should be used instead of "threw" because "through" means "by means of" or "via," which fits the meaning of the sentence.
  • The word "threw" is a past tense form of the verb "throw," which means "to toss or hurl," and it does not fit the meaning of the sentence in this context.

Thus, option 2 is the correct answer.

Correct sentence: As the world becomes more connected through the internet, the importance of cybersecurity has grown exponentially.

textEn:
Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the underlined word.

Everyone liked the lively music being played at the restaurant.
textHi:
Select the most appropriate ANTONYM of the underlined word.

Everyone liked the lively music being played at the restaurant.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Sombre.

Key Points

  • The word "lively" means full of life and energy; active and outgoing. (जीवंत)
    • Example: The lively atmosphere at the carnival made everyone feel happy and excited.
  • "Sombre" refers to something that is dark, dull, or serious; not cheerful or lively. (उदास)
    • Example: The mood at the funeral was sombre, with everyone reflecting on the loss.
  • Hence, we can infer that the opposite of 'lively' is 'sombre'.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Option 4.

Additional Information

Here are the other options explained along with their Hindi meanings and example sentences:

  • Durable (टिकाऊ): Able to withstand wear, pressure, or damage; hard-wearing.
    • Example: The durable fabric of the sofa ensured it lasted for years.
  • Cheerful (प्रसन्न): Noticeably happy and optimistic.
    • Example: Her cheerful demeanor brightened up the entire room.
  • Speedy (तेज़): Moving or capable of moving at high speed.
    • Example: The speedy delivery service ensured the package arrived the next day.

textEn:
Choose the option that best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase underlined in the sentence.
Peter and Ravi agreed to bury the hatchet after a gap of ten years.
textHi:
Choose the option that best expresses the meaning of the idiom/phrase underlined in the sentence.
Peter and Ravi agreed to bury the hatchet after a gap of ten years.
optionsEn: to restore the relationship after a long quarrel,
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Option 1 i.e. to restore the relationship after a long quarrel.

Key Points

  • The phrase "bury the hatchet" means to end a conflict or quarrel and become friendly again. (झगड़ा समाप्त करना और फिर से दोस्त बनना)
    • Example: After years of rivalry, the two companies decided to bury the hatchet and work together.
  • "to restore the relationship after a long quarrel" refers to the act of reconciling after a dispute or conflict. (लंबे झगड़े के बाद संबंध बहाल करना)
    • Example: Peter and Ravi agreed to bury the hatchet after a gap of ten years, deciding it was time to restore their friendship.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Option 1 i.e. to restore the relationship after a long quarrel.

Additional Information

Here are the other options explained along with their Hindi meanings and example sentences:

  • to live happily ever after (सुखपूर्वक जीवन बिताना): Refers to a situation where people live happily for the rest of their lives.
    • Example: The prince and the princess lived happily ever after.
  • to become enemies (दुश्मन बनना): Refers to turning into adversaries.
    • Example: They were once friends, but a dispute over money caused them to become enemies.
  • open for a discussion (चर्चा के लिए खुला): Refers to being willing to discuss or debate something.
    • Example: The manager is always open for a discussion about improving team performance.

textEn:
Change this sentence from active to passive:

"They will have completed the project before the deadline."
textHi:
Change this sentence from active to passive:

"They will have completed the project before the deadline."
optionsEn: The project will have been completed by them before the deadline.
The project will be completed by them before the deadline.
They will have completed the project before the deadline by them.
The project has been completed by them before the deadline.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: 'The project will have been completed by them before the deadline.'

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in Future Perfect Tense and in Active Voice.
  • To change a Future Perfect Tense from Active voice to Passive voice: Interchange the object and the subject with each other - object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence.
  • Structure:
    • Subject + will have + V3 + object (Active Voice)
    • Object + will have been + V3 + by + Subject (Passive Voice)
  • Example:
    • They will have completed the project before the deadline. (Active Voice)
    • The project will have been completed by them before the deadline. (Passive Voice)
  • The subject of the given sentence is 'They.'
  • The object of the given sentence is 'the project.'
  • Apply the rule changes - 'will have completed' will be changed into 'will have been completed'.

The correct answer is option 1.

Correct sentence: The project will have been completed by them before the deadline.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is: 'The project will have been completed by them before the deadline.'

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in Future Perfect Tense and in Active Voice.
  • To change a Future Perfect Tense from Active voice to Passive voice: Interchange the object and the subject with each other - object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive sentence.
  • Structure:
    • Subject + will have + V3 + object (Active Voice)
    • Object + will have been + V3 + by + Subject (Passive Voice)
  • Example:
    • They will have completed the project before the deadline. (Active Voice)
    • The project will have been completed by them before the deadline. (Passive Voice)
  • The subject of the given sentence is 'They.'
  • The object of the given sentence is 'the project.'
  • Apply the rule changes - 'will have completed' will be changed into 'will have been completed'.

The correct answer is option 1.

Correct sentence: The project will have been completed by them before the deadline.

textEn:
Directions: In this section, each sentence is incomplete and is followed by four options. Select one of the four options to complete the sentence.

The government's new policy on renewable energy will only be effective __________
textHi:
Directions: In this section, each sentence is incomplete and is followed by four options. Select one of the four options to complete the sentence.

The government's new policy on renewable energy will only be effective __________
optionsEn: after extensive research and development.
if public support remains consistent.
unless it contradicts previous regulations.
as long as it is accompanied by substantial investment.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is "as long as it is accompanied by substantial investment."


Key Points

  • Implementing renewable energy initiatives typically requires significant financial investment for development, infrastructure, and possibly incentivizing adoption, making option 4 the most logical completion.
  • The phrase "as long as" introduces a condition that is necessary for the effectiveness of the government's new policy, emphasizing that without substantial investment, the policy may fail to achieve its goals.
  • Options 1, 2, and 3 do not provide a condition crucial for the effectiveness of the policy in the same manner option 4 does. They suggest possible factors but do not directly address the necessity for financial backing to ensure implementation and success.
  • Considering the practical aspects of transitioning to or implementing renewable energy policies, significant investment is a realistic and critical requirement, marking option 4 as the most suitable completion.

Hence, the correct answer is 'Option 4'.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is "as long as it is accompanied by substantial investment."

Hence, the correct answer is 'Option 4'.

textEn:
Read the comprehension and answer below:

Independent observers can notice what insiders either miss or quietly refuse to acknowledge. That means drawing on a range of perspectives: philosophy, sociology, psychology, and patients themselves – all of whom may be better placed to ask what medicine is for, how well it works, and who or what can serve its purpose best. In that spirit, I want to open a conversation about the past and future of medicine.

Take prognosis and treatment, where a psychological spotlight on doctors' performance is revealing. Doctors must act with conviction; hesitation can cost lives. Here, unlike philosophers who can hedge with 'on the one hand' and 'on the other', clinicians are forced into swift, high-stakes decisions. Confidence, even overconfidence, is baked into the role. The problem is that confidence is no guarantee of accuracy. One study of intensive-care patients found that doctors who were 'completely certain' of their diagnosis were wrong as much as 40 per cent of the time. Worse still, as experience and familiarity with making clinical judgments increase, doctors tend to consult colleagues less and seek fewer second opinions. Authority can curdle into overconfidence – which can seem like arrogance.

Patients, paradoxically, collude in this. We prefer doctors who project confidence, even if misplaced. The white coat is still treated as a symbol of authority; we are reassured by decisiveness, even when it is wrong. But conviction, as decades of studies show, is an unreliable proxy for accuracy. As one pathologist put it, physicians are 'walking around in a fog of misplaced optimism'.

For centuries, we have lived under the myth of the irreplaceable doctor. Physicians are not only healers but cultural icons: secular priests of the body, guardians of mortality, and interpreters of suffering. We look to them not simply for treatment but for reassurance, ritual, even a touch of transcendence. Yet this mythology clouds judgment. When we insist that 'only a human' can offer care, what we often mean is that we cannot imagine a different arrangement. But history is full of occupations and roles once thought untouchable – clerics, navigators, even bank tellers – that were eventually dethroned in different domains and guises. Then there is symptom denial – not among patients, but in the profession itself. The French Enlightenment philosopher Voltaire mocked his character Dr Pangloss, tutor to the young Candide, who insisted that 'all is for the best' in the 'best of all possible worlds', no matter how dire reality appeared. Medicine has often adopted its own Panglossian stance, downplaying or deflecting recognition of its failures. Diagnostic error, for example, was largely ignored for most of medical history.

What is the meaning of the word "proxy" as used in the passage?

In that spirit, I want to open a conversation about the past and future of medicine.

Take prognosis and treatment, where a psychological spotlight on doctors' performance is revealing. Doctors must act with conviction; hesitation can cost lives. Here, unlike philosophers who can hedge with 'on the one hand' and 'on the other', clinicians are forced into swift, high-stakes decisions. Confidence, even overconfidence, is baked into the role. The problem is that confidence is no guarantee of accuracy. One study of intensive-care patients found that doctors who were 'completely certain' of their diagnosis were wrong as much as 40 per cent of the time. Worse still, as experience and familiarity with making clinical judgments increase, doctors tend to consult colleagues less and seek fewer second opinions. Authority can curdle into overconfidence – which can seem like arrogance.

Patients, paradoxically, collude in this. We prefer doctors who project confidence, even if misplaced. The white coat is still treated as a symbol of authority; we are reassured by decisiveness, even when it is wrong. But conviction, as decades of studies show, is an unreliable proxy for accuracy. As one pathologist put it, physicians are 'walking around in a fog of misplaced optimism'.

For centuries, we have lived under the myth of the irreplaceable doctor. Physicians are not only healers but cultural icons: secular priests of the body, guardians of mortality, and interpreters of suffering. We look to them not simply for treatment but for reassurance, ritual, even a touch of transcendence. Yet this mythology clouds judgment. When we insist that 'only a human' can offer care, what we often mean is that we cannot imagine a different arrangement. But history is full of occupations and roles once thought untouchable – clerics, navigators, even bank tellers – that were eventually dethroned in different domains and guises. Then there is symptom denial – not among patients, but in the profession itself. The French Enlightenment philosopher Voltaire mocked his character Dr Pangloss, tutor to the young Candide, who insisted that 'all is for the best' in the 'best of all possible worlds', no matter how dire reality appeared. Medicine has often adopted its own Panglossian stance, downplaying or deflecting recognition of its failures. Diagnostic error, for example, was largely ignored for most of medical history.

What is the meaning of the word "proxy" as used in the passage?
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Option (3) i.e. Substitute.

Key Points

  • The word "proxy" means a person or thing that acts or stands in for another; a substitute or representative. (प्रतिनिधि / विकल्प)
    • Example: She attended the meeting as a proxy for her manager.
  • "Substitute" is the correct meaning of "proxy" as used in the passage. (विकल्प)
    • Example: In some online systems, you can use a proxy server as a substitute for your real server.
  • Hence, "proxy" means "substitute" in the given context.

Therefore, the correct answer is- Substitute

Additional Information

Here are the other options explained along with their Hindi meanings and example sentences:

  • Tradition (परंपरा): A custom or belief passed down through generations.
    • Example: Lighting lamps is a tradition during Diwali.
  • Guarantee (गारंटी): A formal promise or assurance.
    • Example: The product comes with a two-year guarantee.
  • Obstacle (बाधा): Something that blocks or hinders progress.
    • Example: Fear of failure can be a major obstacle to success.

Which of the following can be concluded from the passage?
optionsEn: Doctors are never wrong when they act decisively.
Overconfidence in medicine is reinforced by both doctors and patients, and the myth of the irreplaceable doctor hinders necessary changes in the field.
Medicine has always openly acknowledged its diagnostic errors.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'Option 3'.
Key Points
  • The passage explains that overconfidence in medicine is not only a problem among doctors (who may consult less as they gain experience) but is also reinforced by patients, who prefer confident doctors.
    • It also discusses the persistent myth of the irreplaceable doctor, which hinders reform and adaptation in the medical field.
  • Option 1: Doctors are never wrong when they act decisively.
    • Incorrect, the passage specifically notes that confidence does not ensure accuracy and that doctors can be wrong even when certain.
  • Option 2: Patients usually distrust confident doctors.
    • Incorrect, the passage says patients are reassured by confidence.
  • Option 4: Medicine has always openly acknowledged its diagnostic errors.
    • Incorrect, the passage notes that diagnostic error was largely ignored for most of medical history.
Therefore, it can be concluded that overconfidence in medicine is reinforced by both doctors and patients, and the myth of the irreplaceable doctor hinders necessary changes in the field.

In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank.

During the Renaissance, artists focused (1)______ depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2)______ to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3)______ religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 1.
textHi:
Comprehension:
In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank.

During the Renaissance, artists focused (1)______ depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2)______ to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3)______ religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 1.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'on'.
Key Points
  • The verb "focused" typically takes the preposition "on" when referring to the object or area of attention.
  • In this context, the artists directed their attention or efforts "on" depicting the beauty of the human form and nature.
  • "Focused on" means to give all your attention or effort to something.
Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 4'.
Complete Passage: During the Renaissance, artists focused 1. on depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They 2. ______ to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift 3. ______ religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, 4. ________ art 5. ______ centuries.
Additional Information
  • At: "Focused at" is less common in this context and usually implies directing something towards a specific point or target, rather than concentrating attention.
  • In: "Focused in" is generally incorrect in this construction; "in" might refer to being inside something.
  • With: "Focused with" would imply using something to focus, which doesn't fit the meaning here.

Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 2.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'aimed'.
Key Points
  • The sentence describes a general action or goal of artists during the Renaissance. The simple past tense "aimed" is appropriate for a completed action in that historical period.
  • "Aimed to capture realism" indicates their consistent objective throughout that era.
Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 3'.

Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Additional Information
  • Option 1: "had aimed" - This is the past perfect tense, which is used for an action completed before another past action. While grammatically possible in some contexts, "aimed" (simple past) fits better for a continuous characteristic of the period rather than an action completed before another specific past event.
  • Option 2: "were aiming" - This is the past continuous tense, which suggests an action ongoing at a specific point in the past. While their aiming was continuous, the simple past "aimed" more concisely conveys their general objective during the Renaissance.
  • Option 4: "was aiming" - This is also past continuous, but "was aiming" is singular, while the subject "They" (referring to artists) is plural. Therefore, it is grammatically incorrect.

explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'from'.
Key Points
  • The sentence describes a "shift" in focus or themes. When indicating a change from one point or state to another, the preposition "from" is typically used with "shift."
  • "Shift from X to Y" is a common and grammatically correct construction to show a transition or change of origin.
Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 3'.
Complete Passage: During the Renaissance, artists focused (1) on depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2) aimed to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3). from religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Additional Information
  • Along: "Shift along" would imply moving physically beside something, which doesn't fit the thematic change.
  • Through: "Shift through" might suggest moving via something or examining things one by one, not indicating a change of origin.
  • Around: "Shift around" would mean moving to different positions, not a directional change from one theme to another.

textHi:
Comprehension:
In the following passage, some words have been deleted. Read the passage carefully and select the most appropriate option to fill in each blank.

During the Renaissance, artists focused (1)______ depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2)______ to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3)______ religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 4.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'influencing'.
Key Points
  • The sentence states that Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo "left an incredible mark" on art. This implies that their contributions had a significant and lasting effect on art that followed.
  • "Influencing" means having an effect on the character, development, or behavior of someone or something. This accurately captures the idea that their work shaped and guided art for future generations.
Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 4'.
Complete Passage: During the Renaissance, artists focused (1) on depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2) aimed to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3) from religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4.) influencing art (5) ______ centuries.
Additional Information
  • Motivate: To motivate means to provide a reason for doing something. Artists motivate *people* to create, but they don't "motivate art" itself in this context.
  • Instill: To instill means to establish (an idea or attitude) in someone's mind gradually. While they might instill values *through* art, they don't "instill art" as a concept or practice in this phrase.
  • Encourage: To encourage means to give support, confidence, or hope to someone. While they encouraged *other artists*, they didn't "encourage art" as a direct object in this way; "influencing" is a more fitting description of their legacy on the art form itself.

The correct answer is 'influencing'.

Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 4'.

  • Instill: To instill means to establish (an idea or attitude) in someone's mind gradually. While they might instill values *through* art, they don't "instill art" as a concept or practice in this phrase.
  • Encourage: To encourage means to give support, confidence, or hope to someone. While they encouraged *other artists*, they didn't "encourage art" as a direct object in this way; "influencing" is a more fitting description of their legacy on the art form itself.

Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4)________ art (5)______ centuries.
Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 5.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'for'.
Key Points
  • The sentence describes the duration or extent of the influence of Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo's art.
  • The preposition "for" is used to indicate a period of time during which something lasts or has been lasting. "For centuries" correctly expresses that their influence extended over many centuries.
Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 1'.
Complete Passage: During the Renaissance, artists focused (1) on depicting the beauty of the human form and nature. They (2) aimed to capture realism, using techniques like chiaroscuro to create depth and shadow. This period saw a shift (3) from religious themes to a celebration of individualism and classical knowledge. Artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo left an incredible mark, (4) influencing art (5.) for centuries.
Additional Information
  • After: "After centuries" would mean their influence started *after* several centuries had passed, which contradicts the idea of them leaving an immediate and lasting mark.
  • By: "By centuries" does not form a coherent phrase to express duration. "By" is typically used to indicate a means, a deadline, or proximity.
  • On: "On centuries" is grammatically incorrect in this context for expressing duration. "On" is typically used for specific days or surfaces.

Mr. Malhotra is a historian / a who's books / b have won international acclaim. / c No error / d
explanationEn:
The correct answer is Option 2.
Key Points
  • The primary issue in the sentence is the misuse of the contraction "who's" which should be "whose".
  • "Who's" is a contraction for "who is" or "who has," whereas "whose" is a possessive pronoun.
  • The correct phrase should be "whose books," ensuring grammatical correctness and clarity.
  • Thus, the error is located in part B of the sentence.
Therefore, the correct answer is B.
Correct Sentence: "Mr. Malhotra is a historian whose books have won international acclaim."
Additional Information
  • A: "Mr. Malhotra is a historian," – This part of the sentence is grammatically correct and does not require any changes.
  • C: "have won international acclaim." – This part of the sentence is grammatically correct and does not require any changes.

textEn:
Directions: Select the correct indirect form of the given sentence.

He said to her, "Please accomplish the task.''
textHi:
Directions: Select the correct indirect form of the given sentence.

He said to her, "Please accomplish the task.''
optionsEn: He requested her that to accomplish the task.
He requested her accomplished the task.
He requested her to accomplishing the task.
He requested her to accomplish the task.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is He requested her to accomplish the task.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is a direct speech.
  • The basic rules for changing or converting direct speech into indirect speech:​
    • The commas, inverted commas are removed.
    • The reporting verbs such as 'said', 'said to' etc. change to ''requested, ordered, suggested and advised''.
    • 'Please' indicates a request and therefore, 'requested' should be used here.
    • The imperative mood in the direct speech changes into the infinitive in the indirect speech.

Hence, the correct answer is option 4.

Confusion Points

  • You may think the past tense form of the verb 'accomplished' should be used.
  • Option 2 is wrong because the past form of the verb cannot be used as the infinitive should be used.
  • Thus, the correct answer is option 4.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is He requested her to accomplish the task.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is a direct speech.
  • The basic rules for changing or converting direct speech into indirect speech:​
    • The commas, inverted commas are removed.
    • The reporting verbs such as 'said', 'said to' etc. change to ''requested, ordered, suggested and advised''.
    • 'Please' indicates a request and therefore, 'requested' should be used here.
    • The imperative mood in the direct speech changes into the infinitive in the indirect speech.

Hence, the correct answer is option 4.

  • You may think the past tense form of the verb 'accomplished' should be used.
  • Option 2 is wrong because the past form of the verb cannot be used as the infinitive should be used.
  • Thus, the correct answer is option 4.

textEn: She said to me, "What are you doing?"
textHi: She said to me, "What are you doing?"
optionsEn: She asked me what was I doing
She asked me what am I doing
She asked me what I was doing
She asked me what were you doing
explanationEn:
The correct answer is Option 3.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in direct speech.
  • The reporting verb of the sentence is in past tense.
  • Therefore the tense of the reported speech will be changed accordingly.
  • Present Continuous Tense changes to Past Continuous Tense.
  • The interrogative sentence changes to a simple sentence in an indirect speech.
  • Thus, helping verb will not be placed before the subject.
  • Conjunction that is not used in conversion interrogative sentence as wh-word acts as conjunction.

She asked me what I was doing is the correct answer.

Hint

  • Option 1 and 2 cannot be our answers as helping verb is placed before the subject of the reported speech.

The correct answer is Option 3.

textEn: Lover of nature is called
textHi: Lover of nature is called
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Option 3) Dendrophile.

Key Points

  • The word "Dendrophile" means a person who loves trees and forests. (वृक्ष प्रेमी)
    • Example: As a dendrophile, John spends most weekends hiking in the forest and observing different tree species.
  • "Dendrophile" refers to a person who has a special affinity for trees. (वृक्ष प्रेमी)
    • Example: Mary is such a dendrophile that she can identify nearly every tree in her neighborhood by its leaves and bark.
  • Hence, we can infer that a lover of nature, particularly trees, is called a 'Dendrophile'.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Option 3) Dendrophile.

Additional Information

Here are the other options explained along with their Hindi meanings and example sentences:

  • Autophile (स्वयं प्रेमी): A person who loves solitude or being alone.
    • Example: As an autophile, she enjoys spending her evenings reading books in her cozy room.
  • Green panther (हरा तेंदुआ): This term is not commonly used to describe a lover of nature and might be more of a creative or fictional term.
    • Example: The term 'Green panther' was used in the novel to describe a nature warrior.
  • Petrichor (पेट्रिचोर): The pleasant, earthy smell after rain.
    • Example: After the long summer drought, the petrichor was a welcome scent in the air.

The newsreader said that he was there for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment.
textHi:
Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.

The newsreader said that he was there for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment.
optionsEn: The newsreader said, "I am here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."
The newsreader said, "I do come here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."

The newsreader said, "I was here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: The newsreader said, "I am here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."

Key Points

  • Option 1 is the correct Indirect Speech because it accurately reports what the newsreader said in the past tense without changing the meaning of the sentence. The verb ""was"" is changed to ""am"" to reflect the present tense in direct speech.
  • Option 2 is incorrect because it changes the tense of the sentence to present continuous, which is not what the newsreader said.
  • Option 3 is incorrect because it changes the tense to present perfect, which is also not what the newsreader said.
  • Option 4 is incorrect because it changes the tense to past tense, which is not what the newsreader said.

Therefore, the correct answer is: The newsreader said, "I am here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."

Key Points

  • Option 2 is incorrect because it changes the tense of the sentence to present continuous, which is not what the newsreader said.
  • Option 3 is incorrect because it changes the tense to present perfect, which is also not what the newsreader said.
  • Option 4 is incorrect because it changes the tense to past tense, which is not what the newsreader said.

Therefore, the correct answer is: The newsreader said, "I am here for a heartfelt apology for making such a comment."

textEn:
Convert this active sentence into a passive one:

"Sarah is baking cookies."
textHi:
Convert this active sentence into a passive one:

"Sarah is baking cookies."
optionsEn: Cookies have been baked by Sarah
Cookies are being baked by Sarah.
Cookies had been baked by Sarah
Sarah's cookies are baking.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: 'Cookies are being baked by Sarah'.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in the Present Continuous Tense in Active Voice.
  • To change a Present Continuous Tense from Active voice to Passive voice, we interchange the subject and the object - the object of the active sentence becomes the subject of the passive one.
  • Structure:
    • Subject + is/am/are + verb+ ing + object (Active Voice)
    • Object + is/am/are + being + past participle + by subject (Passive Voice)
  • In the given sentence:
    • The subject of the sentence is 'Sarah'.
    • The object of the sentence is 'cookies'.
  • Applying the rule changes, 'is baking' will be changed into 'are being baked'.

Correct sentence: Cookies are being baked by Sarah.

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.

textEn:
From the options provided choose the one that best explains the underlined idiom/phrase:

As usual he is blowing his own trumpet.
textHi:
From the options provided choose the one that best explains the underlined idiom/phrase:

As usual he is blowing his own trumpet.
optionsEn: Refusing to use anybody else's trumpet
Playing a tune on the trumpet
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Option 4.

Key Points

  • The phrase "blowing his own trumpet" means to praise oneself, often excessively. (अपनी ही प्रशंसा करना)
    • Example: He never misses an opportunity to blow his own trumpet about his achievements at work.
  • "Praising himself" refers to someone speaking highly of their own abilities or achievements. (स्वयं की प्रशंसा करना)
    • Example: She keeps praising herself for the success of the project, even though it was a team effort.

Therefore, the correct answer is: Option 4.

Additional Information

Here are the other options explained along with their Hindi meanings and example sentences:

  • Praising himself and others (स्वयं और दूसरों की प्रशंसा करना): Refers to someone who praises both themselves and others.
    • Example: He was praising himself and others for the successful event.
  • Refusing to use anybody else's trumpet (किसी और की तुरही का उपयोग करने से इनकार करना): This is a literal interpretation and does not fit the idiom.
    • Example: He refused to use anybody else's trumpet, preferring his own.
  • Playing a tune on the trumpet (तुरही पर धुन बजाना): Literally means playing music on a trumpet.
    • Example: He was playing a tune on the trumpet during the parade.

textEn:
Fill in the blanks using the correct tense (of the verb):

As I _____ (walk) through the park, I ____ (notice) that the birds _____ (stop) singing and suddenly I ____ (feel) a chill. It was as if they ____ (know) something I did not.
textHi:
Fill in the blanks using the correct tense (of the verb):

As I _____ (walk) through the park, I ____ (notice) that the birds _____ (stop) singing and suddenly I ____ (feel) a chill. It was as if they ____ (know) something I did not.
optionsEn: walked; noticed; had stopped; felt; knew
walks; notices; have stopped; feels; knows
explanationEn:
The correct answer is Option(1) i.e. 'walked; noticed; had stopped; felt; knew'.

Key Points

  • The actions occur in sequence in the past, which is indicated by the past simple tense ('walked', 'noticed', 'felt', 'knew').
  • Past perfect tense ('had stopped') is used for an action that had happened before another action in the past (noticing the silence).
  • This sequence of tenses accurately reflects the action sequence and timing as described in the situation.

Therefore, the complete sentence is- As I walked through the park, I noticed that the birds had stopped singing and suddenly I felt a chill. It was as if they knew something I did not.

Additional Information

  • Option(2) "walk; notice; stop; feel; knew" does not correctly sequence the past actions as it mixes the present and past tense.
  • Option(3) "walks; notices; have stopped; feels; knows" incorrectly uses present tense for all actions, which doesn't match the past events described.
  • Option(4) "walking; noticing; stops; feeling; know" presents a continuous or progressive aspect inappropriate for the finished actions indicated.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is Option(1) i.e. 'walked; noticed; had stopped; felt; knew'.

Key Points

  • The actions occur in sequence in the past, which is indicated by the past simple tense ('walked', 'noticed', 'felt', 'knew').
  • Past perfect tense ('had stopped') is used for an action that had happened before another action in the past (noticing the silence).
  • This sequence of tenses accurately reflects the action sequence and timing as described in the situation.

Therefore, the complete sentence is- As I walked through the park, I noticed that the birds had stopped singing and suddenly I felt a chill. It was as if they knew something I did not.

Additional Information

  • Option(2) "walk; notice; stop; feel; knew" does not correctly sequence the past actions as it mixes the present and past tense.
  • Option(3) "walks; notices; have stopped; feels; knows" incorrectly uses present tense for all actions, which doesn't match the past events described.
  • Option(4) "walking; noticing; stops; feeling; know" presents a continuous or progressive aspect inappropriate for the finished actions indicated.

S: The term evolution was first applied by the English philosopher Herbert Spencer to mean the historical development of life.

S6: Philosophers, Thinkers, and Scientists all submitted their varied thought and propositions regarding the nature and mechanism of the origin of life on earth.

textEn:

A. In fact, he began his career as a peon in a small firm.
B. Before he joined us as the accounts officer, he was a junior clerk.
C. He learnt typing, accounting and even graduated.
D. But he gradually improved his qualifications.
textHi:
Directions: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in jumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph.

A. In fact, he began his career as a peon in a small firm.
B. Before he joined us as the accounts officer, he was a junior clerk.
C. He learnt typing, accounting and even graduated.
D. But he gradually improved his qualifications.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'BADC.'

Key Points

  • The sentence 'B' is independent of any other sentence as it is introducing or giving general information about "an accounts officer". Hence, 'B' is the first part.
  • Sentence A will come after B as it is giving information about the early stage career of the accounts officer who began his career as a peon.
  • Sentence D will come after A as it is giving information about how the man became an accounts officer from a peon i.e. by gradually improving his qualifications.
  • Sentence 'C' concludes by saying that the man learnt typing, accounting, and even done graduation to become an accounts officer.

Paragraph after rearranging the sentences: Before he joined us as the accounts officer, he was a junior clerk. In fact, he began his career as a peon in a small firm. But he gradually improved his qualifications. He learnt typing, accounting and even graduated.

Hint

  • Important Rules to Solve the Para Jumbles:
    • Read the sentences attentively and try to comprehend the idea or theme of the sentence and eliminate the irrelevant options.
    • Try to identify the opening (independent sentence) and closing sentences (concluding sentence).
    • Try to identify the mandatory pairs. Two sentences can be connected through various connectors and other determinants.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is 'BADC.'

Key Points

  • The sentence 'B' is independent of any other sentence as it is introducing or giving general information about "an accounts officer". Hence, 'B' is the first part.

  • Sentence D will come after A as it is giving information about how the man became an accounts officer from a peon i.e. by gradually improving his qualifications.
  • Sentence 'C' concludes by saying that the man learnt typing, accounting, and even done graduation to become an accounts officer.

textEn:
Select the most appropriate indirect form of the given sentence.

He said, "Burglars broke into my house last night."
textHi:
Select the most appropriate indirect form of the given sentence.

He said, "Burglars broke into my house last night."
optionsEn: He said that burglars was broken into his house the last night.
He said that burglars had broken into his house the last night.
He asked that burglars had broken into his house the previous night.
He said that burglars had broken into his house the previous night.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'He said that burglars had broken into his house the previous night".
Key Points
  • The given sentence is an example of an assertive sentence.
  • The given sentence is in the form of direct speech. As per the question we have to change it into indirect speech.
  • Basic rules for changing or converting direct speech into indirect speech:​
    • 'Comma' and 'inverted comma' are omitted in indirect speech and are often replaced by 'that' clauses to form 'that clauses'.
    • According to the reporting verb, changes are made in the direct sentence or the sentence in inverted commas.
    • 'Said' will be no change in the indirect speech.
    • 'That' conjunction will be added to account for the comma and double-inverted commas (starting a direct speech)
    • 'burglars' will be no change in the indirect speech. Third-person (he/she/it/they) or any person's name no change.
    • 'broke' will be changed into 'had broken' in the indirect speech. (past simple to past perfect)
    • 'my' will be changed into 'his' in the indirect speech. (first-person > third-person) according to the subject of the reporting verb.
    • 'The last night' will be changed into 'the previous night' in indirect speech. (change of time/adverbs direct form to indirect form)​
  • This is the direct and indirect speech rule for the assertive sentence.
  • Example:
    • ​He said, "His girlfriend came yesterday." (direct speech)
    • He said that his girlfriend had come the day before. (indirect speech)​
  • ​Therefore, as per the points mentioned above, we find that "He said that burglars had broken into his house the previous night" is the correct indirect form of the given sentence.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is 'He said that burglars had broken into his house the previous night".

S1 : When I was a child, my grandfather lived with me and my family.

S6 : He believed that abstaining from speaking for a set period each day brought him inner peace and made him a better listener.

P : Even as the rest of the household stirred awake and the various morning rituals unfolded around him, he stayed still and quiet.

Q : Nothing could detract him during that time.

R : Every morning he would wake up at the crack of dawn and sit in complete silence for an hour.

S : He referred to it as the Mouna Vratham, a ritual of meditative silence long practised in Indian Hindu tradition.

The correct sequence should be:
textHi:
Directions: In this section, each item consists of six sentences of a passage. The first and the sixth sentences are given in the beginning as S1 and S6. The middle four sentences in each have been jumbled up and labelled as P, Q, R and S. You are required to find the proper sequence of the four sentences and mark your response accordingly on the Answer Sheet.

S1 : When I was a child, my grandfather lived with me and my family.

S6 : He believed that abstaining from speaking for a set period each day brought him inner peace and made him a better listener.

P : Even as the rest of the household stirred awake and the various morning rituals unfolded around him, he stayed still and quiet.

Q : Nothing could detract him during that time.

R : Every morning he would wake up at the crack of dawn and sit in complete silence for an hour.

S : He referred to it as the Mouna Vratham, a ritual of meditative silence long practised in Indian Hindu tradition.

The correct sequence should be:
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'RPQS.'

Key Points

  • While arranging the parts of the passage given in options, we have to find some grammatical or contextual connections between them, so let's find out.
    • The first statement in the sequence should be the one that best connects with S1.
    • It should be a continuation or extension of S1.
    • R follows S1 because it is adding information about the daily routine of the grandfather.
    • P follows R because it is adding more information about the grandfather.
    • P must be followed by Q as it is talking about "Nothing could detract him during that time."
    • S is directly connected to S6 as P is talking about the Mouna Vratham ritual that made him a better listener.
  • Therefore, the sequence is 'S1RPQSS6.'
  • Correct sequence:
    ​

S1 : When I was a child, my grandfather lived with me and my family.

R : Every morning he would wake up at the crack of dawn and sit in complete silence for an hour.

P : Even as the rest of the household stirred awake and the various morning rituals unfolded around him, he stayed still and quiet.

Q : Nothing could detract him during that time.

S : He referred to it as the Mouna Vratham, a ritual of meditative silence long practised in Indian Hindu tradition.

textEn:
Select the grammatically correct form of the given sentence from the following options.

The association have been requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
textHi:
Select the grammatically correct form of the given sentence from the following options.

The association have been requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
optionsEn: The association has being requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
The association has been requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
The association has requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
The association has been request the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is "The association has been requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020."

Key Points

  • The error lies in the use of "have" instead of "has".
  • "Association" is a singular noun, hence, it requires a singular verb for agreement.
  • The correct verb phrase for the present perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an action that began in the past and is still continuing, is "has been + present participle (verb-ing)."

Therefore, the correct answer is "option 2".
explanationHi:
The correct answer is "The association has been requesting the government to give the provision of fixing the fees since 2020."

  • The correct verb phrase for the present perfect continuous tense, which is used to describe an action that began in the past and is still continuing, is "has been + present participle (verb-ing)."

Therefore, the correct answer is "option 2".

textEn:
Direction: Choose the appropriate prepositional phrase for the given sentence:

Have you ______ the Aurora Islands?
textHi:
Direction: Choose the appropriate prepositional phrase for the given sentence:

Have you ______ the Aurora Islands?
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'heard of.'

Key Points

  • Let us see the meanings of the given options:
    • Heard of: be aware of or know of the existence of someone or something.
      • Example - Nobody had ever heard of my college.
    • Heard from: If you hear from someone, you get a letter, email, or phone call from that person, or that person tells you something.
    • Heard through: to listen to all of something. (सब कुछ सुनना)
    • Heard over: Incorrect construction
  • The given sentence implies that whether the person was aware of the Aurora Islands.
  • Therefore, the correct answer is Option 1.

​​Correct sentence: Have you heard of the Aurora Islands?

textEn:
Direction: The following item has part of the sentence followed by four alternatives. Complete the sentence by choosing the correct alternative.

A nation reveals itself not only by the men it produces
textHi:
Direction: The following item has part of the sentence followed by four alternatives. Complete the sentence by choosing the correct alternative.

A nation reveals itself not only by the men it produces
optionsEn: if also by the men it honors, the men it remembers
but also by the men it honors, the men it remembers
and also by the men it honors, the men it remembers
or also by the men it honors, the men it remembers
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'but also by the men it honors, the men it remembers.'

Key Points

  • Not only … but also is used to connect and emphasize two phrases or two words at the same position.
  • Different Structures:
    • Subject + Verb + not only + Adjective + but also + Adjective
    • Subject + Verb + not only + Adverb + but also + Adverb
    • Subject + Verb + not only + Noun + but also + Noun
    • Subject + not only + Verb + but also + Verb
      • Example: I like not only cheesecake but also cake.
  • Hence,'but also by the men it honors, the men it remembers' should be used to make the sentence grammatically correct.
  • ​The Correct Sentence: A nation reveals itself not only by the men it produces but also by the men it honors, the men it remembers.

Additional Information

  • A conjunction is a joiner, a word that connects (conjoins) parts of a sentence.
  • Coordinating conjunctions join equals to one another.
    • Example: Most children like sweets and cookies.
  • Coordinating conjunctions go in between items joined, not at the beginning or end.
    • Example: I like coffee, but I don't like tea.

Hinglish

  • Not only … but also एक ही स्थिति में दो वाक्यांशों या दो शब्दों को जोड़ने और उन पर जोर देने के लिए भी उपयोग किया जाता है।
  • विभिन्न संरचनाएं:

    • Subject + Verb + not only + Adverb + but also + Adverb
    • Subject + Verb + not only + Noun + but also + Noun

      • Example: I like not only cheesecake but also cake.

textEn:
Select the correct active form of the given sentence.

I remember being taken to the zoo by my mother.
textHi:
Select the correct active form of the given sentence.

I remember being taken to the zoo by my mother.
optionsEn: I remember my mother were taking me to the zoo.
I remembered my mother taking me to the zoo.
I remember my mother has been taking me to the zoo.
I remember my mother taking me to the zoo.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'I remember my mother taking me to the zoo.'

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in Passive Voice. As per the question we have to change it into Active Voice.
  • ​​The process of transformation of these kinds of sentences as follows:
  • The given sentence is an example of Gerund. Infinitives, gerunds, and participles can also have passive forms.
  • Example:
    • ​I hate people lying to me. (Active Voice)
    • I hate being lied to. (Passive Voice)
  • Here, in the given question 'being taken' will be changed into 'taking'.
  • The progressive form of the verb is changed into 'being + V3' in the 'active to passive' transformation.
  • The object 'My mother' will be used as a subject.
  • The preposition 'by' will be removed.
  • Therefore, as per the points mentioned above, we find that the correct answer is Option 4.
    ​

Correct Sentence: I remember my mother taking me to the zoo.

Additional Information

  • Transformation of Infinitives:
  • Subject+is/am/are/was/were/has/have/had+to+V1+Object.
  • Subject(Objective case)+is/am/are/was/were/has/have/had+to+be+V3+by+object(subjective case).
    • Example:
      • Your child is to learn chess. (Active Voice)
      • Chess is to be learned by your child. (Passive Voice)

​

  • दिया गया वाक्य Passive voice में है। सवाल के अनुसार हमें इसे Active voice में बदलना होगा ।
  • इस प्रकार के वाक्यों के परिवर्तन की प्रक्रिया निम्नानुसार है:
  • दिया गया वाक्य Gerund का एक उदाहरण है। Infinatics, gerunds, और participles के Passive form भी हो सकते हैं।
  • यहां दिए गए प्रश्न में 'being taken' को 'taking' में बदल दिया जाएगा ।
  • Subject के रूप में 'My mother' का उपयोग किया जाएगा।
  • 'By' preposition हटा दिया जाएगा

The correct answer is 'I remember my mother taking me to the zoo.'

Key Points

  • The given sentence is an example of Gerund. Infinitives, gerunds, and participles can also have passive forms.

textEn:
Given below are six sentences, out of which sentences A and F are in the correct position. Sentences B, C, D and E are in a jumbled order. Select the option that gives the correct order.

A. Raja Rammohan Roy is known as the pioneer of women's rights in India.
B. Roy argued that Hindu scriptures did not condone or encourage Sati.
C. Since 1818, Roy wrote in great detail about Sati and how he was pro-abolition.
D. The reason behind this is specifically his support for the abolition of Sati.
E. His writing, however, also focused on reading and interpretation of scriptures.
F. His views resonated with the British argument that widows were coerced into the pyre for material gains.
textHi:

B. Roy argued that Hindu scriptures did not condone or encourage Sati.

explanationEn:

  • Sentence E is the fourth sentence as it mentions another view that his writings focused on.
  • Sentence B is the fifth sentence as it further tells about his writings and his interpretation of Hindu scriptures.
  • Sentence F is the last sentence as per the given instructions. It tells that he urged the British government to abolish Sati.

textEn:
Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.

Vikash said that he would leave for Mumbai the next day.
textHi:
Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.

Vikash said that he would leave for Mumbai the next day.
optionsEn: Vikash said, "I shall leave for Mumbai tomorrow."
Vikash said, "I might leave for Mumbai tomorrow."
Vikash said, "I shall be leaving for Mumbai the next day."
Vikash said, "I should leave for Mumbai the next day."
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: Vikash said, "I shall leave for Mumbai tomorrow."

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in indirect speech, and we need to convert it to direct speech.
  • When we change an indirect speech to direct speech, we must follow these rules:
    • The reporting verb 'said' remains unchanged.
    • The conjunction 'that' is replaced with inverted commas ("...") to show the direct speech part.
    • A comma (,) is added after the direct speech part.
    • The first word of the direct speech is in capital letters.
    • The word 'next day' changes to 'tomorrow'.
    • The modal 'would' changes to 'shall'.
    • The pronoun 'he' changes to 'I'.

Thus, option 1 is the correct answer.

Hence, direct speech is - Vikash said, "I shall leave for Mumbai tomorrow."
explanationHi:
The correct answer is: Vikash said, "I shall leave for Mumbai tomorrow."

    • The word 'next day' changes to 'tomorrow'.
    • The modal 'would' changes to 'shall'.
    • The pronoun 'he' changes to 'I'.

Thus, option 1 is the correct answer.

Hence, direct speech is - Vikash said, "I shall leave for Mumbai tomorrow."

textEn:
Identify the part of the sentence that has an error in it:

He came (a)/ in the (b)/ class without (c)/ the teacher's permission.(d)
textHi:
Identify the part of the sentence that has an error in it:

He came (a)/ in the (b)/ class without (c)/ the teacher's permission.(d)
explanationEn:
The correct answer is '(b)'.
Key Points
  • The phrase "in the" is incorrect in the given sentence.
  • With the verb "came", the correct preposition is "into" when indicating movement toward the inside of a place.
  • Therefore, "came in the class" should be replaced with "came into the class".
  • Options (a), (c), and (d) are grammatically correct in the sentence.
Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.
Correct Sentence: "He came into the class without the teacher's permission."
Additional Information
  • Come in means to enter generally, but when followed directly by a noun/place, "into" is preferred.
  • Example: She came into the room quietly.
  • Example: Please come in.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is '(b)'.
Key Points
  • The phrase "in the" is incorrect in the given sentence.
  • With the verb "came", the correct preposition is "into" when indicating movement toward the inside of a place.
  • Therefore, "came in the class" should be replaced with "came into the class".
  • Options (a), (c), and (d) are grammatically correct in the sentence.
Therefore, the correct answer is Option 2.
Correct Sentence: "He came into the class without the teacher's permission."
Additional Information
  • Come in means to enter generally, but when followed directly by a noun/place, "into" is preferred.

textEn:
Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.

The student said that he had to study hard for the upcoming exam.
textHi:
Select the option that expresses the given sentence in direct speech.

The student said that he had to study hard for the upcoming exam.
optionsEn: The student told, "I must study harder for the upcoming exam."
The student said, "I shall work hard for the upcoming exam."
The student said, "I had to work more hard for the upcoming exam."
The student said, "I must study hard for the upcoming exam."
explanationEn:
The correct answer is: The student said, "I must study hard for the upcoming exam."

Key Points

  • The given sentence is in indirect speech, and we need to convert it to direct speech.
  • When we change an indirect speech to direct speech, we must follow these rules:
    • The reporting verb 'said' remains unchanged.
    • The conjunction 'that' is replaced with inverted commas ("...") to show the direct speech part.
    • A comma (,) is added after the direct speech part.
    • The first word of the direct speech is in capital letters.
    • 'had to' changes to 'must' in direct speech.
    • The pronoun 'he' changes to 'I'.​
  • For converting direct speech into indirect speech, the present modals (e.g., Can, May, Must) are changed into past modals (e.g., Could, Might, Had to).

Thus, option 4 is the correct answer.

Hence, direct speech is- The student said, "I must study hard for the upcoming exam."

Thus, option 4 is the correct answer.

Hence, direct speech is- The student said, "I must study hard for the upcoming exam."

textEn:
Directions: In this section, each sentence is incomplete and is followed by four options. Select one of the four options to complete the sentence.

Despite his promise of arriving on time, he was late for the meeting ______.
textHi:
Directions: In this section, each sentence is incomplete and is followed by four options. Select one of the four options to complete the sentence.

Despite his promise of arriving on time, he was late for the meeting ______.
optionsEn: but he didn't care much.
and nobody seemed to notice.
because traffic was heavier than usual.
so he decided to call ahead.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is "because traffic was heavier than usual".

Key Points

  • The use of "despite" at the beginning of the sentence sets up a contrast or an unexpected result. The phrase "he was late for the meeting" is a consequence that needs a reason that contrasts yet explains the initial action.
  • The conjunction "because" is used to introduce a reason or explanation for something that happened. In this sentence, it explains why he was late despite his promise, linking the cause (heavy traffic) with the effect (being late).
  • The options "but he didn't care much," "and nobody seemed to notice," and "so he decided to call ahead," don't serve to explain the causal relationship between his promise to be on time and his eventual tardiness, making them less appropriate completions for the sentence.
  • Option 3 logically fills the gap by providing a specific reason (heavier than usual traffic) for the consequence mentioned earlier (being late), adhering to the cause-and-effect structure implied by the sentence setup.
Hence, the correct answer is 'Option 3'
explanationHi:
The correct answer is "because traffic was heavier than usual".

  • The use of "despite" at the beginning of the sentence sets up a contrast or an unexpected result. The phrase "he was late for the meeting" is a consequence that needs a reason that contrasts yet explains the initial action.
  • The conjunction "because" is used to introduce a reason or explanation for something that happened. In this sentence, it explains why he was late despite his promise, linking the cause (heavy traffic) with the effect (being late).

  • Option 3 logically fills the gap by providing a specific reason (heavier than usual traffic) for the consequence mentioned earlier (being late), adhering to the cause-and-effect structure implied by the sentence setup.
Hence, the correct answer is 'Option 3'

textEn:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blanks.

The ______, natural way to express anger is to respond aggressively. Anger is a natural, ______ response to threats; it inspires powerful, often aggressive feelings and ______, which allow us to fight and to defend ourselves when we are attacked.
textHi:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blanks.

The ______, natural way to express anger is to respond aggressively. Anger is a natural, ______ response to threats; it inspires powerful, often aggressive feelings and ______, which allow us to fight and to defend ourselves when we are attacked.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'instinctive'.
Key Points
  • The word "instinctive" means relating to or prompted by instinct; done without conscious thought.
  • The word "adaptive" means having the ability to change or be changed to fit altered circumstances.
  • The word "behaviours" refers to the way in which one acts or conducts oneself, especially toward others.
  • The context of the sentence is about natural responses and actions, making option 1 the most appropriate.

​Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 1'.

Complete Sentence: The instinctive, natural way to express anger is to respond aggressively. Anger is a natural, adaptive response to threats; it inspires powerful, often aggressive feelings and behaviours, which allow us to fight and to defend ourselves when we are attacked.

Additional Information
  • Extraordinary (असाधारण): means very unusual or remarkable.
  • Seductive (लुभावनी): means tempting and attractive; enticing.
  • Unreasonable (अतार्किक): means not guided by or based on good sense.
  • Phenomenal (अद्भुत): means very remarkable; extraordinary.
  • Atypical (असामान्य): means not representative of a type, group, or class.
  • Booming (तेजी से बढ़ना): means having a period of great prosperity or rapid economic growth.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is 'instinctive'.
Key Points
  • The word "instinctive" means relating to or prompted by instinct; done without conscious thought.
  • The word "adaptive" means having the ability to change or be changed to fit altered circumstances.
  • The word "behaviours" refers to the way in which one acts or conducts oneself, especially toward others.
  • The context of the sentence is about natural responses and actions, making option 1 the most appropriate.

​Therefore, the correct answer is 'Option 1'.

Complete Sentence: The instinctive, natural way to express anger is to respond aggressively. Anger is a natural, adaptive response to threats; it inspires powerful, often aggressive feelings and behaviours, which allow us to fight and to defend ourselves when we are attacked.

Additional Information
  • Extraordinary (असाधारण): means very unusual or remarkable.
  • Seductive (लुभावनी): means tempting and attractive; enticing.
  • Unreasonable (अतार्किक): means not guided by or based on good sense.
  • Phenomenal (अद्भुत): means very remarkable; extraordinary.
  • Atypical (असामान्य): means not representative of a type, group, or class.
  • Booming (तेजी से बढ़ना): means having a period of great prosperity or rapid economic growth.

textEn: Directions: Which is the correct indirect Speech of the sentence:- Ram says to me ,"you are dull."
textHi: Directions: Which is the correct indirect Speech of the sentence:- Ram says to me ,"you are dull."
optionsEn: Ram said to me that you are dull.
Ram tells me that I was dull.
Ram tells me that I am dull.
Ram told me that I was dull.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is Ram tells me that I am dull​​.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is a direct speech.
  • The basic rules for changing or converting direct speech into indirect speech:​
    • The commas, inverted commas are removed, and 'that' is added.
    • The reporting verb ''says to'' becomes ''tells''.
    • The second-person pronoun ''you'' becomes the first-person pronoun ''I'' and the second-person singular present verb 'are' should be replaced by the first-person singular present 'am'.

Hence, the correct answer is option 3.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is Ram tells me that I am dull​​.

Key Points

  • The given sentence is a direct speech.
  • The basic rules for changing or converting direct speech into indirect speech:​
    • The commas, inverted commas are removed, and 'that' is added.
    • The reporting verb ''says to'' becomes ''tells''.
    • The second-person pronoun ''you'' becomes the first-person pronoun ''I'' and the second-person singular present verb 'are' should be replaced by the first-person singular present 'am'.

Hence, the correct answer is option 3.

textEn:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank.

She ______ the speed of the car before we came to a sharp bend in the road.
textHi:
Select the most appropriate option to fill in the blank.

She ______ the speed of the car before we came to a sharp bend in the road.
explanationEn:
The correct answer is 'had increased'.

Key Points

  • Here, we use the past perfect with the past simple as we are talking about two actions or events in the past.
    • We always use the past perfect for the action that happened first.
    • We can link the two actions using a time expression.
    • After using the past perfect in the 1st part of the sentence, we use simple past in the 2nd part of the sentence.
      • For example:- After she had read all the greetings cards, she made a short thank-you speech.

​​Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Correct sentence - She had increased the speed of the car before we came to a sharp bend in the road.

Additional Information

  • Simple past tense:- The simple past tense shows that you are talking about something that has already happened.
    • Structure:- Subject + V2 + Object + time phrase.
    • For example:- Lisa went to the supermarket yesterday.
  • Past perfect tense:- The past perfect, also called the pluperfect, is a verb tense used to talk about actions that were completed before some point in the past.
    • ​Structure:- Subject + had + V3 + Object.
    • For example:- Daniel had left by the time Erica got to his house.
explanationHi:
The correct answer is 'had increased'.

Key Points

  • Here, we use the past perfect with the past simple as we are talking about two actions or events in the past.

      • For example:- After she had read all the greetings cards, she made a short thank-you speech.

​​Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

Correct sentence - She had increased the speed of the car before we came to a sharp bend in the road.

  • Simple past tense:- The simple past tense shows that you are talking about something that has already happened.
